justification: As the operator, A is more useful despite B's broader source mix and richer pricing detail. A reframes the headline finding — that the 'wave of closures' is largely an artifact of stale Google listings, with only Beggin a genuine recent failure — and ties it to operator-relevant causes (June 2026 road reconstruction, 7-day paid parking, rent ceiling in private tenements). That diagnosis directly informs decisions this week: prepare for street works, lean harder into Wolt, examine the 16% negative-review polarization around pet-friendly seating and biang-biang wait times. B is broader (Trafik, Brassica, Plastry cross-platform arbitrage) and cites more delivery and press domains, but it conflates dead listings with recent closures (claiming Beggin was registered in 2024 contra A's CEIDG-sourced March 2026), skips review_analyst entirely, and stops at observation rather than implication. A's specialist set also matches the rubric expectation; B is missing review_analyst on a query where review trajectories matter.
